epigraph.text: For you formed my inward parts; you knitted me together in my mother's womb. I praise you, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made.
epigraph.quote: The most important questions of life are indeed, for the most part, really only problems of probability.

content.text:
Among all possible distributions, one stands supreme in its importance 
to statistics: the **normal distribution**. Its distinctive 
bell-shaped curve appears throughout nature, from human heights to 
measurement errors, from IQ scores to astronomical observations.

Before defining the normal distribution, we need the concept of a 
**density curve**—a mathematical model for a distribution.

content.title: Density Curve
content.content:
A **density curve** is a curve that:

1. Is always on or above the horizontal axis

2. Has total area exactly 1 underneath it
The area under the curve over any interval represents the proportion 
of observations falling in that interval.

content.text:
A density curve is an *idealized* model. Real data give histograms 
with bars; the density curve is the smooth approximation we would 
approach with infinite data and infinitely narrow bins.

content.title: Normal Distribution
content.content:
A **normal distribution** is described 
by a density curve with the equation
\[
 f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} 
 \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
\]
where $\mu$ is the mean and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation.

We write $X \sim \Normal(\mu, \sigma^2)$ to indicate that $X$ has a 
normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.

content.title: Carl Friedrich Gauss (1777--1855)
content.content:
The normal distribution is sometimes called the "Gaussian distribution" 
after Carl Friedrich Gauss, who used it to analyze astronomical 
observations. Gauss proved that the normal distribution arises 
naturally when averaging many small, independent errors—a mathematical 
result that helps explain its ubiquity in nature. A devout Lutheran, 
Gauss once said, "There are problems to whose solution I would attach 
an infinitely greater importance than to those of mathematics, for 
example touching ethics, or our relation to God."

items: **Bell-shaped:** unimodal with tails extending to infinity

Completely determined by two parameters: $\mu$ (center) and 
 $\sigma$ (spread)

content.title: The Empirical Rule
content.content:
For a normal distribution $\Normal(\mu, \sigma^2)$:

- Approximately **68\%** of observations fall within 
 $\mu \pm \sigma$

- Approximately **95\%** of observations fall within 
 $\mu \pm 2\sigma$

- Approximately **99.7\%** of observations fall within 
 $\mu \pm 3\sigma$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Applying the Empirical Rule
content.problem:
Adult male heights in a population are approximately normal with 
mean $\mu = 70$ inches and standard deviation $\sigma = 3$ inches.
content.solution:
Using the Empirical Rule:

- About 68\% of men are between $70 \pm 3$, or 67 to 73 inches.

- About 95\% are between $70 \pm 6$, or 64 to 76 inches.

- About 99.7\% are between $70 \pm 9$, or 61 to 79 inches.
A man who is 79 inches tall (6'7") would be unusually tall—among 
the tallest 0.15\% of the population.

content.title: Standard Normal Distribution
content.content:
The **standard normal distribution** is 
the normal distribution with mean $\mu = 0$ and standard deviation 
$\sigma = 1$, denoted $\Normal(0, 1)$.

The standard normal variable is typically denoted $Z$.

content.text: Any normal distribution can be transformed to the standard normal.

content.title: $z$-Score
content.content:
The **$z$-score** of an observation $x$ 
from a distribution with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ is
\[
 z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}
\]
The $z$-score tells how many standard deviations $x$ lies from the mean.

content.title: Finding Probabilities
content.problem:
SAT scores are approximately normal with $\mu = 1060$ and $\sigma = 200$. 
What proportion of students score above 1400?
content.solution:
First, standardize:
\[
 z = \frac{1400 - 1060}{200} = \frac{340}{200} = 1.70
\]
We need $P(Z > 1.70)$. From the standard normal table or technology:
\[
 P(Z > 1.70) = 1 - P(Z \leq 1.70) = 1 - 0.9554 = 0.0446
\]
About 4.5\% of students score above 1400.

content.text:
We can also work backward: given a percentile, find the corresponding 
value.

content.title: Finding a Percentile Cutoff
content.problem: What SAT score marks the 90th percentile?
content.solution:
First, find the $z$-score with area 0.90 to its left. From the table 
or technology: $z \approx 1.28$.

Then convert back to the original scale:
\[
 x = \mu + z\sigma = 1060 + 1.28(200) = 1060 + 256 = 1316
\]
A score of about 1316 marks the 90th percentile.

content.text:
Not all data are normally distributed. Before using normal-based 
methods, we should check whether the normal model is reasonable.

items: **Histogram:** Does it look roughly bell-shaped and symmetric?

**Empirical Rule check:** Do roughly 68\% of values fall 
 within one standard deviation of the mean?

**Normal probability plot (Q-Q plot):** If data are normal, 
 points should fall approximately on a straight line.

marginNotes.text:
For his invisible attributes, namely, 
his eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, 
ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made.
